actions.thought: J7o is a marginal but playable hand in a heads-up situation when on the button. Since I have positional advantage for the remainder of the hand, I will put in a standard raise to 3x the big blind to exert pressure and potentially take the pot down immediately or play a controlled pot in position.

I am holding Jh 3c in the Big Blind facing a 3x raise (to 300) from the Small Blind. To call, I need to contribute 200 chips into a pot that will be 600, giving me 3:1 pot odds (requiring ~25% equity). While my raw equity against a wide stealing range might arguably meet this threshold, J3o is a trash hand with very poor playability (reverse implied odds, domination issues). Furthermore, I will be playing the entire post-flop hand out of position. Against a larger-than-minimum raise size of 3bb, defending with the bottom of my range is not profitable. Folding preserves my stack early in the match.

I am on the Button (SB) in a Heads-Up match with 6c 3h, which is a very weak hand (63o). In Heads-Up No-Limit Hold'em, the Button typically opens a wide range (often 80%+), but 63o falls into the bottom 10-20% of hands that are generally folded. It lacks connectivity and high card strength, making it difficult to play profitably even with positional advantage. Folding is the standard and most prudent play here to avoid bleeding chips with garbage hands.

Holding 6h 5d on the button in a heads-up situation, I want to maintain an aggressive posture. While the hand is relatively weak, connectivity gives it some post-flop potential, and opening from the small blind/button is standard for a wide range of hands. I will raise to 300, consistent with my action in Hand #1, to exert pressure and attempt to take the blinds.

I am in the Big Blind with Pocket Sixes (6d 6s) facing a 3x open raise from the Button (Flash). In heads-up play, pocket pairs are strong hands with good equity against a wide opening range. Getting 3:1 pot odds (calling 200 to win 600), I require only 25% equity, and 66 has significantly more than that against a standard button range. While a 3-bet is an option to deny equity to overcards, calling is the standard low-variance play to keep the opponent's range wide and avoid getting blown off the hand by a 4-bet. Being out of position post-flop, I will flat call and evaluate the board.

I am out of position on a paired board of Jh-Js-7d with pocket sixes (6d 6s). This board texture interacts reasonably well with a Button opening range (which contains many Jx hands), but also offers many opportunities for the opponent to c-bet light. My hand has showdown value, beating Ace-high and random floats. Leading out (donk betting) would likely only get action from better hands or fold out hands I am currently beating. The standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor to induce bluffs or control the pot size.

The board texture of 7d Jh Js is very dry and favors the pre-flop raiser's range, which includes many high cards and potential trips. Although I have missed the board completely with 6h 5d, this is a prime opportunity for a continuation bet. A small bet of 200 (1/3 the pot) puts pressure on the opponent's marginal holdings and allows me to take down the pot without much risk. If called, I will likely slow down unless the turn improves my hand or provides a better bluffing card.

I hold Pocket Sixes (6d 6s) on a dry, paired board of J-J-7. The opponent has c-bet 200 into a pot of 600 (1/3 pot), offering me 4:1 odds (requiring 20% equity). In heads-up play, a small c-bet on a paired board is extremely common with a very wide range, including air, overcards, and marginal hands. While I am behind any Jack or higher pocket pair, I am ahead of all Ace-highs and unpaired broadway hands that bluff this texture. Folding here would be over-folding against a standard c-bet frequency. Raising turns my hand into a bluff that isolates me against better hands. The best play is to call the small bet to keep their bluffing range wide and re-evaluate on the turn.

The Turn card is the Ace of Hearts (Ah), making the board 7d Jh Js Ah. This is a terrible card for my hand (Pocket Sixes). With the board containing J-J-A-7, my sixes are effectively counterfeited; my best five-card hand is now pair of Jacks with A-7-6 kickers. Any opponent holding a card higher than a 6 (which is almost their entire range including K-high, Q-high, etc.) now beats me due to the board's high cards. Additionally, the Ace interacts very strongly with the opponent's pre-flop raising and flop c-betting range. I have negligible showdown value and am out of position. Leading out would be throwing chips away. The only viable option is to check and prepare to fold to a bet, hoping the opponent checks back.

The Turn card is the Ace of Hearts, which is an ideal card for my perceived range as the pre-flop aggressor. Since I missed the board entirely with 6-high, my best chance to win the pot is to continue the story I started on the flop. By betting here, I am representing that the Ace helped my hand (AX) or that I already had a strong hand like a Jack. This 'double barrel' should force my opponent to fold most marginal hands like a 7 or small pocket pairs that might have called the flop.

The Turn card is the Ace of Hearts, resulting in a board of Jh-Js-7d-Ah. This is a catastrophic card for Pocket Sixes. My actual five-card hand is now a Pair of Jacks with Ace, Seven, Six kickers. On this board, any opponent holding a card higher than a Six (which includes almost their entire opening range like Kx, Qx, Tx, 9x, 8x) has me beat due to the strength of the board cards (J-J-A-7). I am losing to all value hands (Ax, Jx, 7x, pocket pairs) and I am essentially losing to almost all bluffs (King-high, Queen-high, etc.). Facing a half-pot bet of 500, I need 25% equity to call, but I likely have near 0% equity against the Button's range. I am drawing dead or near-dead. This is an easy fold.

In Heads-Up play, the button (Small Blind) should open a wide range of hands (often 70%+). J3 suited is a marginal but playable hand, with about 52% equity against a random hand. Having folded my previous button opportunity (Hand #2), I want to avoid establishing an overly tight image that allows the opponent to over-fold me or steal my blinds relentlessly. A min-raise to 200 allows me to take the initiative, utilize my position post-flop, and potentially take down the pot pre-flop if the opponent folds weak holdings.

I am holding 9-2 offsuit in the Big Blind. This is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. My opponent in the Small Blind has raised to 200, requiring me to call another 100 chips. Although the pot odds are 3:1, playing this hand out of position (OOP) against a 'Pro' level opponent is generally a losing strategy over the long term. Defending with the absolute bottom of my range is unnecessary at this stage of the tournament with 100BB stacks. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am holding 4c 2s on the button (Small Blind). This is a very weak hand, sitting at the bottom of my range. While playing aggressively from the button is standard in heads-up play, 42o has very little equity and poor playability post-flop. Given I have a slight chip lead and have been active in previous hands, I will choose to fold this hand to maintain my stack and wait for a better opportunity.

I am on the Button (Small Blind) with 8h 4c, a very weak offsuit hand. While I have position post-flop, the hand has very poor playability and low equity against any reasonable calling range. Against an aggressive opponent who has shown a willingness to raise pre-flop and barrel post-flop, limping or raising with this trash hand is likely -EV. Folding is the standard and prudent play here to preserve chips.

K5o is a solid candidate for an open-raise from the button in heads-up play. It has good high-card value against the opponent's defending range and allows me to maintain aggressive pressure. I will raise to 3x the big blind as I have done in previous hands.

I am in the Big Blind with Kd2d facing a 3bb raise from the Button (Flash). In heads-up play, ranges are wide, and a suited King has sufficient equity to defend against an opening range, even out of position. I need to call 200 into a pot of 400, offering me 33% pot odds. My hand has approximately 50% equity against a wide button opening range. Folding would be too tight, and 3-betting is a bit ambitious with the kicker strength, so calling is the standard play.

On a paired board of [9s 9h 7d], as the pre-flop caller out of position, the standard play is to check to the aggressor. I have King-high (Kd 2d) with a backdoor flush draw. The board texture doesn't heavily favor either range, but checking preserves my range and allows me to evaluate the opponent's action. Leading out serves little purpose here.

The flop of 9s 9h 7d is relatively dry and favors the pre-flop aggressor's range. Since Pro checked, I will place a continuation bet of 200 (1/3 of the pot) to exert pressure and take down the pot. King-high still holds some showdown value, but betting here helps deny equity to Pro's random overcards and maintains my table image as an aggressive player.

Facing a small continuation bet of 200 into a pot of 600 (33% pot) on a paired board of [9s 9h 7d]. I am getting 4:1 pot odds, needing only 20% equity to call. In Heads-Up play, ranges are wide, and the opponent likely c-bets this texture with their entire range or a very high frequency. My King-high (Kd2d) is currently ahead of all their pure bluffs (like Q-high, J-high, suited connectors that missed) and I have approximately 35-50% equity against a realistic betting range. Additionally, I hold a backdoor diamond flush draw and an overcard (King). Folding King-high to a 1/3 pot bet on this texture would result in over-folding. Raising turns a hand with showroom value into a bluff unnecessarily. Calling is the most +EV line.

On the turn, the board reads [9s 9h 7d 5h]. The 5h introduces a backdoor heart flush draw, while my hand (Kd2d) remains King-high with no strong draws (only 3 diamonds total). As the caller out of position, the standard line is to check to the pre-flop aggressor who c-bet the flop. I have some showdown value against pure air (like QJ or JT), but betting myself (donk betting) would likely only fold out hands I beat and get action from better hands. I check to see if the opponent slows down or continues aggression.

On the turn 5h, I have improved to a pair of fives with a King kicker. While this is a made hand, it is relatively marginal on a board of 9-9-7-5. Pro has called a pre-flop raise and a flop continuation bet, suggesting they likely have a piece of the board (a 7 or a 9) or a drawing hand. By checking back here, I control the size of the pot and realize my showdown value. If I bet, I am unlikely to get called by worse hands (except perhaps some draws) and I risk being check-raised by a 9 or a 7. Checking allows me to see the river for free and potentially win at showdown if Pro is holding missed overcards.

I arrive at the river with King-high (Kd2d) on a board of [9s 9h 7d 5h 8c]. The action went check-call on the flop and check-check on the turn. The opponent's check-back on the turn signals weakness (pot control with a pair or giving up on a bluff) or a trap, but most likely a marginal hand or Ace-high. The river 8 completes a straight for any 6x. My King-high has very little showdown value, beating only missed straight draws (like QJ, JT) or lower random air. Betting here turns my hand into a bluff, but it is difficult to represent a strong hand (like a 9 or a straight) credibly after checking the turn, and an opponent with Ace-high or a pair (5x, 7x, TT) will likely call a reasonable bet in a Heads-Up match. Checking allows me to get to showdown cheaply if the opponent checks back, or easily fold if they bet, as I cannot call a bet with just King-high. Therefore, checking is the best risk-adjusted play.

On the river 8c, the board is 9-9-7-5-8. I have a pair of fives with a King kicker. Pro has checked twice (turn and river), which often indicates weakness or a marginal hand like a 7 or an 8. Since I have some showdown value with my pair and it's unlikely I can fold out a better hand (like a 7 or 8) with a bet, checking back is the safest and most logical play. I'll take my chances at showdown.

I hold Q4o in the Small Blind (Button). In Heads-Up No-Limit Hold'em, this hand is on the borderline of playability. While I have position, Q4o has poor playability and is dominated by many of the opponent's calling hands (Qx, pairs, Ax). Looking at my previous button actions, I have folded very weak hands (63o, 48o) and raised suited trash (J3s). Q4o is marginally better than the folds but still weak. To avoid bleeding chips in marginal spots early in the tournament (Hand 8/150) and to maintain a slightly stronger range, I will fold this hand.

Holding Jd7d on the button in a heads-up situation is a strong candidate for a raise. This hand has good post-flop playability due to being suited. My strategy so far has involved raising to 300 from the button to put pressure on the big blind. Pro has shown a willingness to fold to aggression in previous hands, and even if called, I will have positional advantage for the rest of the hand.

I hold Pocket Eights (8c 8d) in the Big Blind. The opponent on the Button (SB) has raised to 3bb (300). This is a strong hand heads-up, typically well ahead of a standard button opening range. I have two main options: 3-bet or call. 3-betting builds a pot out of position and might fold out the hands I dominate while isolating myself against overcards or higher pairs. Calling allows me to keep the opponent's range wide, play a controlled pot, and evaluate the board texture, which is generally safer given I will be acting first post-flop. Given the stack depth (88bb) and the opponent's 3x sizing, flat calling is a prudent, standard play to realize equity and bluff-catch or hit a set.

I have flopped a middle set on a relatively dry board (Js 8h 2c). This is an extremely strong hand, effectively the nuts at this point unless the opponent holds specifically Pocket Jacks. Being out of position as the pre-flop caller, standard strategy dictates checking to the pre-flop aggressor. Leading out (donk betting) would likely fold out the opponent's air and deny them the opportunity to make a continuation bet with a wider range. I want to allow the opponent to bluff or value bet a Jack. Therefore, I will check with the intention of either check-raising or check-calling depending on their bet size and action.

I have hit top pair with my Jack on a dry board of Js-8h-2c. Since Pro checked to me, I will place a continuation bet to extract value from weaker hands like mid-pairs (8x) or straight draws (T9, Q9) and to protect my current lead. A half-pot bet of 300 is standard and effective here.

I hold a set of Eights on a relatively dry, rainbow board (Js 8h 2c). This is an incredibly strong hand (second nuts), and I do not need to worry heavily about draws. The opponent has c-bet 300 into a pot of 600. If I check-raise now, I risk folding out all of the opponent's bluffs and weak hands, effectively isolating myself against a range that I crush but that won't pay off further if scared away. By flat calling, I keep the opponent's range wide, allowing them to continue bluffing on the turn or to potentially hit a second-best hand (like top pair or two pair). I will slowplay this street to maximize value on later streets.

I hold a set of Eights (middle set) on a J-8-2-6 board. The pot is 1200. I called a c-bet on the flop. Now out of position on the turn, checking is the standard play to disguise the strength of my hand. I want to encourage the opponent to fire a second barrel (double barrel) either as a bluff or for value with hands like Top Pair (AJ, KJ) or overpairs (QQ, KK, AA). Leading out (donk betting) here would likely fold out their bluffs and define my hand strength too early. If they bet, I can decide whether to check-raise for value or just call to trap, depending on the sizing.

I have top pair (Jacks) with a 7 kicker. The board is relatively safe, and Pro's check on the turn suggests they are in check-call mode or have a weak hand. I will bet 600, which is half the pot, to get value from middle pairs like 8x, smaller pocket pairs, or straight draws like T9. This keeps the pot growing while I likely have the best hand.

I hold a set of Eights on a dry J-8-2-6 rainbow board, which is effectively the second nuts (losing only to JJ). The opponent has fired a second barrel of half-pot (600). On such a dry texture, a check-raise would appear extremely strong and likely fold out the opponent's entire bluffing range and many marginal value hands (like weak Jacks). By flat calling, I underrepresent my hand strength, keeping their range wide and giving them the opportunity to fire a third barrel on the river or call a value bet. There are very few dangerous river cards, so slowplaying is the optimal line to maximize value.

I hold a set of Eights on a board of J-8-2-6-7. The river 7 completes a possible straight for T9 (10-9-8-7-6 is not the straight, 7-8-9-10-J is). T9 is a plausible hand for the opponent (open-ended on the flop). However, I still beat the vast majority of their value range (Overpairs like AA/KK/QQ, Top Pair AJ, etc.) and all bluffs. I have taken a passive line (check-call) on the flop and turn. Donk betting the river now would look very strong and likely fold out their bluffs while only getting action from hands that beat me (straights, higher sets) or strong value hands that would likely bet anyway. By checking, I give the opponent a chance to fire a third barrel as a bluff or a value bet with Top Pair or Overpairs. If they bet, I can decide whether to call or raise based on sizing, but leading out denies them the chance to bluff.

I have improved to two pair (Jacks and Sevens) on the river. While the seven on the river completes a potential straight for T9, my hand is still very strong in a heads-up context. Pro has check-called on every street, which is consistent with a Jack with a better kicker, a middle pair like 8x, or a draw that has now potentially hit or missed. I will bet 1200, which is half the pot, to extract value from weaker Jacks, pocket pairs, or an 8 that might feel compelled to call. If Pro raises, I will have to evaluate the strength of their line, but a value bet is the standard play here.

I hold a set of Eights on a board of J-8-2-6-7. The river 7 completes the straight for T9, which is a very plausible hand given the opponent's triple-barrel line (gutshot on flop, open-ended on turn). While I beat the vast majority of their value range (Overpairs like AA/KK, Top Pair AJ, and lower sets like 77/66/22), the straight presence makes raising dangerous. A raise would likely fold out hands I beat (like AJ or one-pair hands) and isolate me against T9 or JJ which crush me. Calling guarantees I capture value from their overpairs, top pairs, and bluffs, while minimizing losses if they have the straight. The bet size of 1200 is reasonable to call.

I hold 4s 9d on the Button (Small Blind), which is an extremely weak hand (bottom 10-15% of hands). While I have the chip lead and will have position post-flop, this hand has very poor playability due to being unsuited and disconnected. I have established a pattern of folding junk hands from the button (Hand 2, Hand 6, Hand 8), and there is no strategic reason to deviate here. Preserving the stack is preferable to bleeding chips with trash.

I have Pocket Queens, which is a premium starting hand. I want to build a pot and extract value from my opponent. A standard raise to 300 has been my consistent open size, and it is appropriate here to narrow the field and take control of the hand while holding a significant equity advantage.

The opponent is opening to 3x (300 chips) from the Button/Small Blind, which they have done consistently. I am in the Big Blind with 86o, a weak offsuit hand. While I have some raw equity against a wide opening range, I am getting 2:1 pot odds rather than the more favorable 3:1 offered by a min-raise. Being out of position with a hand that has poor playability and reverse implied odds makes equity realization difficult. Against a 3x open size, 86o is below the standard defense threshold. Therefore, folding is the prudent play to preserve chips.

I hold 3c 2s, which is statistically the worst starting hand in Texas Hold'em (3-2 offsuit). Although I am on the button (Small Blind) in a heads-up match, the equity of this hand is extremely low. The cost to complete is minimal (50 chips), but playing this hand profitably is difficult even with position post-flop. Given we are early in the match and I have a chip lead, there is no strategic incentive to get involved with garbage hands. The standard and correct play here is to fold.

I am on the button with 7-4 offsuit. While this is a weak hand, heads-up strategy dictates opening a wide range from the small blind to capitalize on positional advantage. My opponent's previous fold of QQ to a standard pre-flop raise suggests they may be playing overly tight or cautious, making a steal attempt highly profitable. I will raise to 300 to continue applying pressure.

I hold 2c 3s in the Big Blind, which is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em due to poor playability and low equity. The opponent (Flash) has raised to 300 (3x the big blind) from the Button. While Flash is opening frequently, 3-2 offsuit does not have enough equity or playability to defend profitably against a 3x raise out of position. The pot odds are 200 to call into a total pot of 600 (33%), but my realization of equity will be very low. Therefore, the correct decision is to fold.

I am on the Button (SB) in a Heads-Up match with 4c2c. In this format, the SB is in position post-flop but acts first pre-flop. Looking at my history, I have been playing very tight from the SB, folding hands like Q4o, 94o, and 32o (Hands 2, 6, 8, 10, 12). While 42s has some playability due to being suited, it is at the very bottom of the starting hand range. Against an opponent who has shown aggression (Flash consistently raises 3x from the button), opening with 4-high is risky. Although my fold frequency is currently high, 42s is not the hand to correct it with, especially compared to the Q4o I previously folded. Preserving my chip lead (10950 vs 8900) by avoiding marginal spots with trash hands is a sound strategy.

I am on the button with T8o, which is a standard opening hand in heads-up play. Given my history in this session of raising to 300 from the button and seeing a mix of folds and calls from Pro, I will maintain my consistent sizing. This hand has decent playability and serves to put pressure on the big blind.

Opponent 'Flash' is opening frequently (3x raises) from the Button. As the Big Blind holding Q5s, I have a suited Queen which plays reasonably well post-flop and has enough equity (approx. 40-47% against a wide range) to defend against a single raise. The price of calling 200 to win a pot of 600 offers 3:1 pot odds (actually 2:1 on the call money, needing 33% equity), making this a profitable call despite being out of position post-flop.

I hold middle pair (Fives) with a Queen kicker and a backdoor flush draw on a K-5-2 board. The board texture favors the pre-flop aggressor's range (which contains many Kings), but my hand has showdown value against their wider opening range (air, smaller pairs). Donk betting serves little purpose here as it folds out bluffs I beat and isolates me against better hands. Checking is the standard line to induce bluffs or control the pot size. I intend to check-call a reasonable continuation bet given my pair and backdoor equity.

I am the pre-flop aggressor on a dry, King-high board that significantly favors my perceived opening range as the button. Pro has checked to me on the flop, which is consistent with a wide defense range. This is an ideal board for a continuation bet; I will bet 200 chips (one-third of the pot) to fold out Pro's missed hands and marginal cards while representing the King.

I hold middle pair (fives) with a good kicker and a backdoor flush draw. The opponent, who has been aggressive, makes a standard small continuation bet (1/3 pot) on a King-high dry board. This texture interacts well with his range, but he also bets this with air. I am getting excellent pot odds (200 to win 1000, requiring 20% equity) and my hand is well ahead of his bluffs and has equity against top pair. Folding is too weak, and raising isolates me against better hands. Calling is the correct play.

I hold a pair of Fives (now 3rd pair) with a Queen kicker on a K-J-5-2 board. I am out of position against an aggressive opponent who raised pre-flop and c-bet the flop. The Turn Jack interacts well with the opponent's likely range (broadway cards, Kx, Jx). I have 3 clubs (2 in hand, 1 on board), so the flush draw is dead. My hand serves as a bluff-catcher. Leading out (donk betting) would likely isolate me against better hands and fold out the bluffs I beat. The standard and optimal line is to check to the aggressor, aiming to get to showdown cheaply or evaluate facing a bet.

On the turn Jack of Spades, the board is now Kc Js 5s 2h. I have Ten-high with no real draw. Pro checked to me after calling my flop continuation bet. While the Jack is a card that could have helped Pro's floating range (like QJ or JT), it also fits into my perceived range as the pre-flop aggressor. However, since my hand has zero showdown value and I've already been called on a dry board, I will check back to control the pot size. This allows me to see a river card for free and potentially bluff on a favorable river or simply give up the hand if Pro leads out strongly, as my equity is currently very low.

I hold a pair of Fives on a K-J-5-3-2 board. The opponent checked back the turn, which caps their range and suggests they likely hold a marginal hand (weak pair, Ace high) or a missed draw. Betting here serves little purpose as I am unlikely to get called by worse hands (like Ace high) and would only isolate myself against better hands (Jx, Kx) that checked back for pot control. Checking is the optimal play to induce bluffs from missed draws or simply get to showdown cheaply with my marginal showdown value.

The river is the 3c, making the final board Kc Js 5s 2h 3c. I have Ten-high with zero showdown value. The action went: I raised pre-flop, bet the flop, and we both checked the turn. Pro has checked the river as well. Since Pro checked the turn and river, their range likely consists of weak pairs (like 5x or 2x), missed draws, or possibly a Jack that is playing cautiously. Given that I have no chance of winning at showdown, I will make a bet of 600 (60% of the pot) to represent the King I've been signaling since pre-flop. This size puts significant pressure on Pro's marginal holdings that might have called a smaller bet.

On the River, the pot is 1000 and the opponent bets 600. I hold a pair of Fives (3rd pair) with a Queen kicker. The opponent's line of raising pre-flop, betting the flop, checking the turn, and betting the river is often consistent with a marginal made hand (like Jx or a weak King) trying to extract thin value or a 'stab' at the pot after I checked twice. While my hand beats pure bluffs (like missed straight or flush draws), it loses to the vast majority of the opponent's value range. The Turn check-back on a Jack is particularly concerning as it connects with many broadway hands in the opponent's range. Without a strong read that the opponent over-bluffs in this specific 'bet-check-bet' spot, and considering I am near the bottom of my bluff-catching range (I would prefer to call with Kx or Jx), preserving chips is the prudent long-term play. The price of 27% equity is likely not met.

I am on the Button (Small Blind) with Q9o. In Heads-Up No-Limit Hold'em, this hand is well within the top 50% of hands and is too strong to fold. My recent history shows I have been folding my button frequently (hands 8, 10, 12, 14 were all folds with trash hands). Therefore, a raise here is likely to be effective as I haven't been opening light recently. I will make a standard min-raise to 200 to apply pressure and play the pot in position.

I am in the Big Blind holding 8d 5h. Pro has raised to 200 from the Small Blind, requiring me to call 100 more to see a flop. While 85 offsuit is a relatively weak hand, in a heads-up situation with pot odds of 3:1 (100 to win a 300 pot), it is often defendable. However, Pro has been playing somewhat selectively. Given the stack depths and the fact that I am out of position for the rest of the hand, I will elect to call and see if I can connect with the flop, as the price is quite cheap.

I have middle pair (eights) on a Queen-high board. Since I am the first to act on the flop and Pro was the pre-flop aggressor, the standard play is to check. This allows me to see how Pro reacts to the board. If Pro bets, I have a clear call given the strength of my pair in a heads-up pot, but leading out here would likely only get called by better hands or fold out worse ones.

I have flopped Top Pair (Queens) with a decent kicker (9) on a very dry, rainbow board (Q-8-2). My hand is well ahead of the opponent's checking range, which likely includes middle pairs (8x), bottom pairs (2x), pocket pairs (33-77), and various unconnected high cards. A bet here serves to extract value from these worse hands and denies free equity to random overcards (like Kx or Ax) that missed. Checking back is an option for pot control, but betting 50% of the pot builds value immediately while the board is safe.

I have middle pair (Eights) on a relatively dry board of Queen-Eight-Deuce. Pro made a standard continuation bet of 200 into a 400 pot. With middle pair, I have sufficient equity to continue, especially in a heads-up pot. Raising would likely only be called by better hands (like a Queen or an overpair) and fold out hands I currently beat. Therefore, calling is the most profitable action to keep the pot manageable and see how the turn develops.

I have third pair (Eights) on a board that now contains two overcards, a Queen and a Jack. The Jack of Clubs also completes some potential draws or creates new ones. As I am first to act on the turn and was the caller on the flop, the standard play is to check to the aggressor. This allows me to control the pot size and see how the opponent reacts. Leading out would not accomplish much as I am unlikely to get better hands to fold or worse hands to call.

On the Turn, the Jack of Clubs is a dynamic card. It completes the T9 straight and brings a club flush draw, while also giving any Jx hands a pair. Although I still hold Top Pair with a 9 kicker, my hand is vulnerable and not strong enough to confidently bet for three streets of value. Betting here risks isolation against better hands (like QJ, sets, or the straight) or facing a check-raise which would put me in a tough spot. Checking back controls the pot size, induces bluffs on the river from missed draws, and allows me to comfortably call a river bet or value bet on a safe river card against weaker pairs like 8x or Jx.

On the river, the board is 8s 2h Qc Jc 6c. I have third pair with eights. The turn went check-check, which suggests that Pro might not have a very strong hand like a Queen. However, with the Jack on the turn and the third club on the river, my hand has limited value. Checking is the best option here to try and get to a cheap showdown.
